observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to comply with Chapter I, Section 10 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume II on the reconciliation of SL balances with the controlling accounts and immediately address the noted discrepancy of P635,997.04. Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to: a. Trace the variance of P743,271.35 between the Subsidiary ledger (SL) and Cashbook and effect the necessary adjustments; and b. Conduct regular reconciliation of their balances so that the adjustments made and/or errors detected by either of the offices will be adjusted / taken up in their respective books to ensure the correctness of the balance reported as Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor is amenable and assured strict compliance of the audit recommendations. Further, she directed the Municipal Accountant to immediately address the discrepancy noted by reconciling the records with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.
observations.reason: The new Municipal Accountant is still adjusting to his duties and responsibilities, thus the said audit recommendation was not given priority.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to issue demand letter to officials and employees with unliquidated cash advance and request the immediate settlement thereof. Moreover, we recommended that the OIC – Municipal Accountant facilitate the documents needed to request the write-off of the unliquidated cash advances from deceased accountable officers and that the Municipal Mayor to exhaust legal and administrative remedies provided under Section 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-004 due to the failure of the accountable officers to liquidate the cash advances granted to them.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor is firm with her stand that the Municipality will exhaust legal action on the collection of the outstanding cash advance of Mr. Joel E. Cuento totalling P2,857,163.60. Further, the Municipal Accountant requested for a copy of the required documents and procedures to facilitate the request for authority to write-off the receivables from the Accountable Officers that were reported deceased.
observations.reason: The new Municipal Accountant is still adjusting to his duties and responsibilities, thus the said audit recommendation was not given priority.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the portion of the long term loan which is due for settlement within twelve months from reporting date as current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to resolve the issues pertaining to the delayed distribution and ensure the prompt release of the Real Property Tax (RPT) shares to the Barangays on a quarterly basis within five (5) days after the end of each quarter in strict compliance with Section 271(d) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

Inventory Committee to (i) conduct the physical count of inventories and property, plant and equipment (PPE) in accordance with Section 124 of Volume I of Manual on NGAS for LGUs and submit the RPCI and RCPPE to the Audit Team within the timeframe provided by the same regulation; and (ii) prepare the RPCI and RPCPPE accurately by accomplishing all the required data such as the columns for article, unit of measure and shortage/overage on quantity and value and the remarks portion specifically the conditions of the inventories and PPE at time of the physical count;

Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books to correct all the erroneous recording/classification noted and henceforth, ensure proper recording/classification of the properties in accordance with the Chart of Accounts provided in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; and 




Municipal Treasurer to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPE in the IIRUP which could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant made the following adjusting entries to correct the erroneous recording/ classification noted in the AOM:

JEV No. 100-2104-063B dated April 15, 2021 and JEV Nos. 100-2105-086B, 100-2105-087B and 100-2105-088B dated May 17, 2021.
observations.reason:
The municipality was still not able to conduct the physical count of inventories and property, plant and equipment for CY 2021 and thus, they also failed to submit the RPCI and RCPPE to the audit team.

The municipality was still not able to prepare and submit the report of unserviceable PPE or IIRUP.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to 

(a) facilitate the request for writing-off of the dormant and unsubstantiated accounts to present fairly the financial condition of the Municipality; 

(b) provide an allowance for impairment on receivable accounts based on analysis of collection rating and collectability of the accounts; and 

(c) maintain subsidiary records to support the general ledger accounts to avoid the incurrence of the same deficiency.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the:
a. Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to:
 conduct complete physical count for all the property owned by the Municipality;
and
 disclose all necessary information required such as the quantity on hand and the
condition of the PPE items, whether serviceable or not, at the time of physical
count in the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment
(RPCPPE).
b. Municipal Accountant and MGSO to:
 facilitate reconciliation of records to address the noted variance between the
records maintained by respective offices; and
 strictly comply with all the pertinent rules and regulations as prescribed under
COA Circular No. 2020-006.
It is likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to immediately address the noted abnormal balance amounting to P50,775.43 through
the conduct of a thorough review of the account to determine the cause/s of the
abnormal balance and effect necessary corrections and/or adjustments, if warranted.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to:
a. trace the material variance of P5,206,113.32 between the Subsidiary Ledgers (SL)
and Cashbook balances and draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to effect the
necessary adjustment to accounts; and
b. conduct regular reconciliation of their balances so that the adjustments made
and/or errors detected by either of the offices will be adjusted / taken up in their
respective books to ensure the correctness of the balance reported as Cash in
Bank – Local Currency, Current and Savings Accounts, in compliance with
Section 111 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445; and
c. provide a written justification on the noted repeated non-compliance with the
previous years’ audit recommendation; otherwise, administrative sanctions shall
be imposed pursuant to Section 127 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
appropriate collections equally between the Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special
Education Tax (SET) accounts and draw the necessary entries to adjust the balances
of the accounts.

It is likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer to conduct analysis and reconciliation of records to establish the nature of
discrepancy noted between the Subsidiary Ledgers maintained by the Municipal
Accounting Office and Certified Statement of Delinquency submitted by the Municipal
Treasurer’s Office amounting to P14,995,220.67 and make the necessary adjustments
in the RPT/SET Receivables accounts and its Deferred Income by drawing a Journal
Entry Voucher (JEV), if warranted.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant to determine and address the cause/s of the noted
discrepancies between the amounts presented in the Statement of Comparison of
Budgeted and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) and the Statement of Financial Performance
and to disclose the same in the Notes to Financial Statement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to draw
the following correcting journal entry:
Account Debit Credit
Loans Payable – Domestic P495,489.53
Government Equity P495,489.53

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the portion of the long term loan which is due
for settlement within twelve months from reporting date as current liabilities in the
Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 on the required
analysis of dormant accounts by performing the regular and periodic verification,
analysis and validation of the existence of the receivables;
b. provide allowance for impairment for dormant receivables in order to achieve fair
presentation of the accounts presented in the financial statements as required
under International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 1 and 29;
c. coordinate with the Municipal Legal Officer to exhaust legal remedies due to the
failure of the accountable officer to liquidate the cash advances granted to him; and
d. strictly comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No.
2016-005 for the possible request for the authority to write-off the specified
dormant receivable accounts to the Commission.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant to fully disclose the required financial information in the
Notes to Financial Statement to achieve fair presentation of the Financial Statements,
as well as, assist its users in analyzing and understanding the financial statements
pursuant to the pertinent provisions of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all payments be made through
checks in compliance with Section 93 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445, so as to
enable the government to withhold taxes therefrom, if necessary. It is likewise
recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer and the Chairman of the
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) ensure strict compliance with the rules,
regulations, policies and procedural guidelines provided in Republic Act (RA) No.
9184 on the procurement process.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to deny
request for additional cash advance if the Disbursing Officer (DO) has an existing
unliquidated cash advances and strictly monitor the grant and liquidation of the
same. It is likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Treasurer to ensure that only duly appointed or designated disbursing officers will
perform disbursing functions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Accountant, adhere to the internal control set forth in Item 4.1.6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 by not granting lump sum cash advances for the purpose of transferring them to other AOs and in Item 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 by requiring the AOs to liquidate their cash advances on time. Send notice or demand letter to delinquent AOs, and furnish a copy to the audit team.

Thus, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies (e.g., information campaign) to intensify the collection of RPT and SET and to persuade taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount.
Thus, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct a physical count, then prepare and submit the RPCI and RPCPPE to the audit team within the set timeframe.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
The GFPS to explain the late implementation or release of the financial assistance under the Economic Support Services for Women program charged to the GAD Fund and apprise the Audit Team of any subsequent actions taken for its implementation; and
The Municipal Accountant to (i) explain the recording of Accounts Payable for the unreleased and the seemingly disbursed financial assistance; (ii) adjust the books to correct the overstatement; and (iii) present fairly the account balance in the FS.

Thus, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to revisit the balances of the Due to NGAs account and identify which among the unexpended balances are pertaining to completed and fully paid projects. Then, return the unexpended funds to its source agencies and secure an official receipt for it.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and Municipal Engineer to submit the approved Plans and Drawings; corrected ABC; enhanced POW, PERT-CPM and PCAB License; STE supporting the certification; and explain the reason why the LCBs were declared as LCRBs despite the noted errors and deficiencies;
Require the BAC to submit timely the contracts and its supporting documents; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit the revised CAF; cause the submission of the noted lacking document for the payments made; and ensure henceforth that the supporting documents attached to the vouchers are complete and proper before processing of claims.

With that, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to (a) properly observe the provisions of DPWH D.O. No. 197 dated October 7, 2016 in the ABC preparation for future infrastructure projects; (b) review thoroughly the ABC, POW and Detailed Engineering Estimates to ensure that the basis and ceiling of bid prices are acceptable, reasonable and advantageous to the government; and (c) submit written comments on the noted variances otherwise, deduct from the excess amount from the succeeding claims of the contractor.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the BAC to (i) provide to the audit team documents pertaining or relevant to test analysis and the brand name that has been agreed upon by the parties; and (ii) ensure henceforth that the form being used as a binding agreement between the parties contains details (e.g., brand name, delivery terms) indispensable to the transaction; and
Together with the LDRRMO and Municipal Budget Officer, (i) explain the charging of traveling expenses and repairs and maintenance to the budget item for the purchase of rescue vehicle and (ii) ensure henceforth that the usual planning and budgeting processes laid down for special trust fund (i.e., LDRRMFIP, AIP and SB Resolution) are adhered to.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Together with the Municipal Engineer/OIC-MPDC, fast track the implementation of the identified PPAs under the Development Fund and henceforth see to it that all PPAs are provided with target date of implementation/completion in the APP and AIP; and
Apprise the audit team of the new PPAs funded by the funds sourced from the realignment of the above two projects by submitting pertinent documents such as but not limited to Supplemental APP or AIP and Sangguniang Bayan Resolution.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Together with the GAD Focal Person and Municipal Budget Officer,      (i) explain the computation of attributed amount in the GPB and the existence of attributed program but with appropriations under the GAD Fund and (ii) henceforth make sure that the attributed amount is accurate and the GAD Budget is allocated using any or a combination of GAD Fund and attributions;
Instruct the GAD Focal Person to accomplish the GAD Agenda and GMEF Organizational Assessment Questionnaire and furnish a copy to the audit team; and
Instruct the GAD Focal Person to submit the GPB and GAR to the audit team on time.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Together with the Municipal Vice Mayor and Municipal Budget Officer, explain the existence of the budget item Financial Assistance under the Legislative Department; and
Henceforth require the Municipal Budget Officer to be circumspect in reviewing the contents of the budget proposal from each department and see to it that the proposed PPAs are aligned with their functions and mandate.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to require the MA to (a) facilitate the request for writing-off of the dormant and unsubstantiated accounts to present fairly the financial condition of the Municipality; (b) provide an allowance for impairment on receivable accounts based on analysis of collection rating and collectability of the accounts; and (c) maintain subsidiary records to support the general ledger accounts to avoid the incurrence of the same deficiency.

2.3.    We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) create an Inventory Committee to undertake the conduct of physical inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality and prepare the RPCPPE for reconciliation with the MAO records; and  (b) require the MA and GSO to maintain the appropriate property records such as Stock Cards and Property Cards, respectively.
We commended the LCE for the favorable revenue performance of the Municipality and recommended that the LCE require the MT to continuously improve taxes collection through conduct of tax campaigns.

We recommended and the LCE concurred to (a) optimally utilize  the 20% DF  to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU; (b)  direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) observe strictly the general guidelines on the information and publicity of infrastructure projects undertaken by the Municipality in accordance with  COA Circular No. 2013-004; (ii) verify the completeness of technical documents supporting the implementation of infrastructure projects pursuant to the documentary requirements provided by the DPWH Standard Specifications for Highways, Bridges and Airport; and (iii) require the concerned Contractors to  repair the defective and damaged portion of the road projects pursuant to the one year warranty period  from the date of project completion up to final acceptance or the defects liability period pursuant to Section 62 of the CY 2016 IRR of RA No. 9184; and (c) require the MA to ensure the completeness of supporting documents prior to payment.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) MA to submit to the AT the lacking documents to support the validity and propriety of the disbursements; and (b) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the MA to comply with the posting and reporting requirements, respectively, as prescribed under RA Nos. 11469 and 11494, and DBM LBC No. 125.

6.6 	We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Municipal Public Information Office to comply with the mandatory posting of the Report on Fund Utilization of LGSF-FA in the Agency website and in at least three (3) conspicuous places within the Municipality pursuant to DBM LBC No. 136 dated March 30, 2021.
7.6	We recommended and the LCE agreed to continuously require the MA, MT and other concerned Municipal officials to be compliant with the rules and regulations on the use of funds.

8.5	We recommended and  the LCE agreed to (a) require the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officers to  involve closely the barangay officials in  activating the Solid Waste Management Committees in every barangay for the strict implementation of the provisions of RA No. 9003 particularly on maintenance of a  functional MRF and observance of waste segregation at source; (b) consider extending financial assistance and other resources to two  barangays with no sufficient fund to establish their own MRF; and (c) develop programs on composting, recycling, re-use, and recovery of solid waste generated in order to reduce hauling and disposal costs of the Municipality.

9.7 	We recommended and the LCE concurred to (a) require the GAD Focal Point Officer(s) and the MBO to appropriate at least 5% for GAD-related activities; and (b) require the GAD Focal Point Officers to (i) restate the unimplemented PPAs in the preparation of GPB for the ensuing year; and (ii) prepare and submit to the AT the GAD AR within the prescribed period.

10.3 	We recommended and the LCE concurred to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with the withholding of appropriate taxes and remittance thereof to the BIR within the prescribed period.

11.5  	We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with the withholding of GSIS premiums from the employees’ compensation and remittance thereof to the GSIS within the prescribed period.

12.4	We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with regulations on the withholding of premiums and loan amortizations from employees’ compensation and the remittance thereof together with the employer’s share to the HDMF within the prescribed period.

13.4 	We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with the regulations on the withholding of monthly PhilHealth premiums and remittance thereof together with the employer’s share within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to: a. Conduct complete physical count for all the property owned by the Municipality; and b. Maintain Property PCs for PPE and Cards (PCs) for each Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) of the Municipality and reconcile with the ledger cards of the Municipal Accounting Office; and c. Disclose all necessary information required in the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to ascertain the accuracy and reliability of the report.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Inventory Committee conducted a partial physical count of the properties for CY 2021 and submitted its report thereon to the Office of the Auditor on January 31, 2022. Continuous updating of records of both offices to reconcile the balances is being undertaken. Also, the new Municipal Accountant is still adjusting to his duties and responsibilities, thus the said audit recommendation was not given priority.
observations.reason: Due to the pandemic, manpower is limited, thus implementation of the audit recommendation was not facilitated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Engineer to prepare the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects and provide the reporting the same to the OIC – Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO); b. MGSO to prepare and submit the following:  the Report on the Local Road Network (RLRN) in the format prescribed in Annex A to the COA Circular;  the Local Road Inventory and Road Map;  the Local Road Network Property Card (LRNPC) in the format prescribed in Annex E of the Circular; and  the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System (RPCRNS) in the format prescribed in Annex C of the Circular; and c. OIC – Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network system in the Notes to Financial Statements in the format prescribed in Annex B of the audit Circular.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal General Services Officer prepared and submitted its report on the Local Road Network (RLRN) in the format prescribed in Annex A to the Office of the Auditor on January 31, 2022. The Municipal General Services Officer maintained the Stock Cards (SCs) for the supplies and materials procured during the year. The Municipal General Services Officer furnished the OIC – Municipal Accountant with the SSMI.
observations.reason: Due to the pandemic, manpower is limited, thus implementation of the audit recommendation was not facilitated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to ensure that all receipts and utilization of government fund are being recorded in the Municipality’s book of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The pertinent transactions pertaining to the construction of the Farm to Market Road being implemented jointly by the Municipal Government of Santa Maria and the Department of Agriculture (DA) in the trust fund books of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: a. Reconciliation of actual amounts on comparable basis to the budget and actual amounts in the financial statements was not disclosed neither on the face of the Statement of Comparison of Budgeted and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) nor in the Notes to Financial Statements which resulted in a noted net discrepancy of P30,230,691.04, thus posing doubt on the reliability of the reported account balances. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to determine and address the cause/s of the noted discrepancies between the amounts presented in the Statement of Comparison of Budgeted and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) and the Statement of Income and Expense (SIE) and to disclose the same in the Notes to Financial Statement. b. There was no disclosure provided for the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) amounting to P1,183,764.47 as of year-end, contrary to Section 5.1.16 of COA Circular No. 2012-002, thus additional relevant information for future decision making could not be provided to the Management and other users of the financial reports. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant provide the necessary disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements for the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the Fund pursuant to Section 5.116 of COA Circular No. 2012-002. c. Significant information to support the balance of some accounts presented in the financial statements were not disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements, contrary to Paragraphs 127 (c), 128 and 129 of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 1, thus depriving the users of the financial statements of additional relevant information on the affected accounts. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to disclose the required financial information in the Notes to Financial Statement to assist its users in analyzing and understanding the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant assured compliance with the audit recommendation and committed to reconcile the noted discrepancies between the Statement of Comparison of Budgeted and Actual Amounts and the Statement of Financial Performance on or before June 13, 2022. The Municipal Accountant provided the disclosures on the appropriation and utilization of the LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements for Calendar Year (CY) 2021. The Audit Team furnished the Municipal Accountant with the pro-forma Notes to Financial Statements based on the requirements of IPSAS 1 and other pertinent COA Circulars on June 6, 2022.
observations.reason: No reason cited by the Management on its inaction with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. OIC – Municipal Accountant to refrain from recording the procurement or receipt of supplies and materials to expense account, but instead coursed them thru the process inventory account in consonance with the Perpetual Inventory System of accounting; b. OIC – Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs) and Stock Cards (SCs), respectively; c. Preparation of SSMI which shall be the basis of the OIC – Municipal Accountant to record the cost of issued items; d. MGSO to furnish the OIC – Municipal Accountant with copies of the weekly SSMI for recording in the books the cost of issued items; and e. OIC – Municipal Administrator to create Inventory Team who shall conduct the required semi-annual actual count of inventory items.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal General Services Officer started to maintain Stock Cards (SCs) for the supplies and materials procured during the year. The Municipal General Services Officer furnished the Office of the Municipal Accountant the SSMI. No inventory was conducted during CY 2021; consequently, the required Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) was not facilitated.
observations.reason: Delays were encountered by both offices due to the pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to reclassify the progress billings to the appropriate Construction in Progress account and subsequently transfer the project cost to the appropriate Property, Plant and Equipment account, once completed.
observations.managementAction: Progress payments for infrastructure projects were duly transferred to the appropriate Construction in Progress (CIP) account by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer, at the beginning of the year, to furnish the OIC – Municipal Accountant the updated duly certified list of Real Property Tax (RPT) Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card and the corresponding amount due and collectible for the year. Thereafter, based on the submitted list, direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to set-up the RPT/Special Education Tax (SET) Receivables and its corresponding deferred income account by drawing the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV). We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records and make necessary adjustments, if any, to present fairly the accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: On-going reconciliation and validation of records is being undertaken by both offices to locate and address the discrepancy noted.
observations.reason: Due to the pandemic, manpower is limited, thus full implementation of the audit recommendation was not facilitated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct OIC – Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review of the account Due to PhilHealth and determine the cause/s of the abnormal balance and effect necessary corrections and/or adjustments, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant conducted a review of the Due to Philhealth account and effected the necessary corrections and/or adjustments in the books of account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to submit the Disbursement Vouchers and their supporting documents immediately to the Office of the Auditor within the reglementary period prescribed under aforementioned Circulars. It was further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately submit the original copies of the 28 cancelled checks for Calendar Year (CY) 2020 to the Office of the Auditor and henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of Chapter 3, Section 59 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I on the proper handling and accounting of spoiled or cancelled checks.
observations.managementAction: The pertinent Disbursement Vouchers for Calendar Year (CY) 2020 and its supporting documents were submitted to the Office of the Auditor. The Municipal Treasurer submitted the original copies of the 28 cancelled checks for Calendar Year (CY) 2020 to the Office of the Auditor. Cancelled and/or spoiled checks for Calendar Year (CY) 2021 were not duly submitted to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: No reason was cited by the Municipal Treasurer on its inaction with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as the head of the procuring entity (HoPE) and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman to explain in writing the deviation of the Municipality from provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 and other governing rules and regulations in the procurement of goods and services. It is further recommended that the Municipal Mayor to immediately stop the practice of procurement thru cash advance and strictly adhere to the provisions provided in RA No. 9184 to promulgate competitiveness in the procurement process of the Municipality and that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking documents to support the payments to avoid audit suspension and/or disallowance.
observations.managementAction: A written explanation was submitted to the Office of the Auditor. The Management stopped the practice of procurement thru cash advance. The pertinent Disbursement Vouchers and supporting documents were submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the submission of the Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting documents to Office of the Auditor within the reglementary period prescribed under aforementioned Circulars.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office duly observed the reglementary period in the submission of the Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster and Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to prepare and submit to the Office of the Auditor the revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) approved by the Sangguniang Bayan for the use of accrued unexpended balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) utilized by the Municipality to address the disaster response requirements of the COVID-19 emergency. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider maintaining an official website for the Municipality to further promulgate transparency in government procurements by posting awarded contracts, Notice of Award and other related documents and information on the procurement of the goods and services and construction of infrastructure assets. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit the revised Annual Procurement Plan (APP) to the Office of the Auditor and to comply with posting requirements in the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) online portal.
observations.managementAction: The Management considers the launching and maintenance of its own website. The Municipal Disaster and Risk Reduction and Management Officer prepared the revised AIP and submitted the same to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor, through the SWMB, fast track the processing of various requirements for the operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41 of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time to avert adverse environmental consequences.
observations.managementAction: The management said that the road going to the proposed site for sanitary landfill has been coordinated with the provincial government because of the prospect of connecting the LGUs of Santa Maria and Odiongan through said access road at Sitio Aurora, Brgy. Pato-o, Odiongan.
observations.reason: The municipality was still not able to establish a sanitary landfill. They are still in the process yet of application for Environmental Compliance Certificate (ECC).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Assessor and other personnel concerned to submit the copies of the abovementioned required documents to the Audit Team to establish the propriety and validity of the above transactions.
observations.managementAction: A copy of Tax Declaration No. 390 for Lot No. 398-PART was secured by the LGU and furnished to the audit team.
observations.reason: Other than the Tax Declaration, no other documentary requirements were submitted by the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) strictly observe the existing rules and regulations governing collections and deposits of government funds; (b) together with all the collecting officers, strengthen the internal control in cash management by observing the proper cut-off in the reporting of collections, particularly the preparation of Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD); (c) instruct all the collecting officers to exercise caution in the issuance and cancellation of Official Receipts pursuant to Section 2 of Presidential Decree No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Issued Office Order No. 1 s. 2021 requiring all of the accountable officers to remit cash collections twice a week (Monday and Thursday), previously once a week (Thursday), and deposit the collections every Friday.
observations.reason:
Verification of the remittances of collections revealed that some collectors were still not able to remit their collections within the prescribed cut-off in said Office Order.

However, the audit team still underpins that Section 29 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I requires the collectors to remit “at the close of each business day” not just twice a week.

observations.managementAction:

The list of projects and activities charged against the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years was incorporated in the AIP for CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer (MT) to continuously improve taxes collection through conduct of tax campaigns.

We recommended and the LCE concurred to (a) direct the Municipal Engineer to

(i) observe strictly the general guidelines on the information and publicity of infrastructure projects undertaken by the Municipality in accordance with  COA Circular No. 2013-004; 

(ii) verify the completeness of technical documents supporting the implementation of infrastructure projects pursuant to the documentary requirements provided by the DPWH Standard Specifications for Highways, Bridges and Airport; and 

(iii) require the concerned Contractors to  repair the defective and damaged portion of the road projects pursuant to the one year warranty period  from the date of project completion up to final acceptance or the defects liability period pursuant to Section 62 of the CY 2016 IRR of RA No. 9184; and 

(b) require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents prior to payment.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the 

(a) MA to submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents to support the validity and propriety of the disbursements; and 

(b) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the MA to comply with the posting and reporting requirements, respectively, as prescribed under RA Nos. 11469 and 11494, and DBM LBC No. 125.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA, MT and other concerned Municipal officials to continue to be compliant with the rules and regulations on the use of funds.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to 

(a) require the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officers to  involve closely the barangay officials on solid waste management activities and activate the Solid Waste Management Committees in every barangays for the strict implementation of the provisions of RA No. 9003 particularly on maintenance of  functional MRF and observance of waste segregation at source; 

(b) consider extending financial assistance and other resources to two  barangays with no sufficient fund to establish their own MRF; and 

(c) develop programs on composting, recycling, re-use, and recovery of solid waste generated in order to reduce hauling and disposal costs of the Municipality.

We recommended and the LCE concurred to 

(a) require the GAD Focal Point Officer(s) and the MBO to continue appropriating at least 5% for GAD-related activities; and

(b) require the GAD Focal Point Officers to (i) restate the unimplemented PPAs in the preparation of GPB for the ensuing year; and (ii) prepare and submit to the Audit Team the GAD AR within the prescribed period.
We recommended and the LCE concurred to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with the withholding of appropriate taxes and remittance thereof to the BIR within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with the withholding of GSIS premiums from employees’ compensation and remittance thereof to the GSIS within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with regulations on the withholding premiums and loan amortizations from employees’ compensation and remittance thereof together with the Municipal share to the HDMF within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA and the MT to continue to be compliant with the regulations on the withholding of monthly PhilHealth premiums and remittance thereof together with the Municipal share within the prescribed period.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the MA to be more diligent in evaluating the supporting papers on claims to avoid suspension or disallowance of transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to classify and present the current portion of the long-term loans payable in the amount of P697,228.89 as part of the current liabilities and the remaining amount which is due for settlement beyond one year as non-current liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services
Officer (MGSO) to:
a. Conduct complete physical count for all the property owned by the Municipality;
b. Maintain Property Cards (PCs) for each Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) of the
Municipality and reconcile with the ledger cards of the Municipal Accounting Office; and
c. Disclose all necessary information required in the Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to ascertain the accuracy and
reliability of the report.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal
Accountant to update the PPE Ledger Cads (PPELCs) and reflect the PPE items
beyond CY 2018 to facilitate reconciliation of records with the MGSO.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Engineer to prepare the complete description and cost segregation of
road components for road projects and provide the same to the OIC – Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO);
b. MGSO to prepare and submit the following:
 the Report on the Local Road Network (RLRN) in the format prescribed in
Annex A of the COA Circular No. 2015-008
 the Local Road Inventory and Road Map
 the Local Road Network Property Card (LRNPC) in the format prescribed in
Annex E of the Circular and
 the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System (RPCRNS) in
the format prescribed in Annex C of the Circular; and
c. OIC – Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network system in the
Notes to Financial Statements in the format prescribed in Annex B of the
Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant
to comply with Chapter I, Section 10 of the Manual on the New Government
Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume II on the
reconciliation of SL balances with the controlling accounts and immediately
address the noted discrepancy of P635,997.04.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Trace the variance of P743,271.35 between the Subsidiary ledger (SL) and
Cashbook and effect the necessary adjustments; and
b. Conduct regular reconciliation of their balances so that the adjustments made
and/or errors detected by either of the offices will be adjusted / taken up in
their respective books to ensure the correctness of the balance reported as
Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC – Municipal
Accountant to ensure that all receipts and utilization of government fund are
recorded in the Municipality’s book of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant
to determine and address the cause/s of the noted discrepancies between the
amounts presented in the Statement of Comparison of Budgeted and Actual
Amounts (SCBAA) and the Statement of Income and Expense (SIE) and to disclose
the same in the Notes to Financial Statement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant
provide the necessary disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements for the
amount and details of the unexpended balance of the Fund pursuant to Section 5.116
of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the OIC – Municipal Accountant to disclose the required financial information in the
Notes to Financial Statement to assist its users in analyzing and understanding the
financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC – Municipal Accountant
to issue demand letter to officials and employees with unliquidated cash advance and
request the immediate settlement thereof. Moreover, we recommend that the OIC –
Municipal Accountant facilitate the documents needed to request the write-off of the
unliquidated cash advances granted to the deceased accountable officers amounting
to P374,594.00.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor to exhaust legal and
administrative remedies provided under Section 9.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-004 due
to the failure of the accountable officers to liquidate the cash advances granted to
them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. OIC – Municipal Accountant to refrain from recording the procurement or receipt
of supplies and materials to expense account, but instead coursed them thru the
inventory account in consonance with the Perpetual Inventory System of
accounting;
b. OIC – Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to
maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs) and Stock Cards (SCs), respectively;
c. Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to assess if additional workforce
would be necessary to facilitate the implementation of the issuance of Requisition
Issuance Slip (RIS) and its subsequent consolidation and preparation of the
weekly Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI);
d. MGSO to furnish the OIC – Municipal Accountant with copies of the weekly SSMI
for recording in the books the cost of issued items; and
e. OIC – Municipal Administrator to create Inventory Team who shall conduct the
required semi-annual actual count of inventory items.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
reclassify the portion of the long term loan which is due for settlement within twelve
months from reporting date as current liabilities in the Statement of Financial
Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to
reclassify the progress billings to the appropriate Construction in Progress account
and subsequently transfer the project cost to the appropriate Property, Plant and
Equipment account, once completed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer, at the
beginning of the year, to furnish the OIC – Municipal Accountant the updated duly
certified list of Real Property Tax (RPT) Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card and
the corresponding amount due and collectible for the year. Thereafter, based on the
submitted list, direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to set-up the RPT/Special
Education Tax (SET) Receivables and its corresponding deferred income account by
drawing the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records and address the discrepancy of
P27,746,268.72 noted between their records and make necessary adjustments, if any,
to present fairly the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct OIC – Municipal Accountant to
conduct a thorough review of the account Due to PhilHealth and determine the
cause/s of the abnormal balance and effect necessary corrections and/or
adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to
submit immediately to the Office of the Auditor the Disbursement Vouchers and their
supporting documents to ascertain the validity and propriety of transactions and to
avoid audit suspension of transactions, which could mature into disallowance.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
immediately submit the original copies of the 28 cancelled checks to the Office of the
Auditor. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of Chapter 3, Section 59 of the
Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government
Units (LGUs), Volume I on the proper handling and accounting of spoiled or cancelled
checks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as the head of the procuring entity
(HoPE) and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman to explain in writing the
deviation of the Municipality from provisions of Republic Act (RA)No. 9184 and other
governing rules and regulations in the procurement of goods and services.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor to immediately stop the
practice of procuring goods and services through cash advance. Further, we
recommend that the head of the BAC to strictly adhere to the provisions provided in
RA No. 9184 to promulgate competitiveness in the procurement process of the
Municipality.

It is further recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking
documents to support the payments of the subject disbursements, to avoid audit
suspension and/or disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to
strictly observe the submission of the Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting
documents to Office of the Auditor within the reglementary period prescribed under
aforementioned Circulars.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to consider maintaining an official
website for the Municipality to further promulgate transparency in government
procurements by posting awarded contracts, Notice of Award and other related
documents and information on the procurement of the goods and services and
construction of infrastructure assets. Further, we recommend that the Municipal
Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit the revised
Annual Procurement Plan (APP) to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant
and Municipal Treasurer to resolve the issues pertaining to the delayed distribution
and ensure the prompt release of the Real Property Tax (RPT) shares to the
Barangays on a quarterly basis within five (5) days after the end of each quarter in
strict compliance with Section 271(d) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Inventory Committee to (i) conduct the physical count of inventories and property, plant and equipment (PPE) in accordance with Section 124 of Volume I of Manual on NGAS for LGUs and submit the RPCI and RCPPE to the Audit Team within the timeframe provided by the same regulation; and (ii) prepare the RPCI and RPCPPE accurately by accomplishing all the required data such as the columns for article, unit of measure and shortage/overage on quantity and value and the remarks portion specifically the conditions of the inventories and PPE at time of the physical count;
Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books to correct all the erroneous recording/classification noted and henceforth, ensure proper recording/classification of the properties in accordance with the Chart of Accounts provided in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; and
Municipal Treasurer to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPE in the IIRUP which could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) strictly observe the existing rules and regulations governing collections and deposits of government funds; (b) together with all the collecting officers, strengthen the internal control in cash management by observing the proper cut-off in the reporting of collections, particularly the preparation of Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD); (c) instruct all the collecting officers to exercise caution in the issuance and cancellation of Official Receipts pursuant to Section 2 of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended the following:
The Municipal Accountant (i) be guided by the requirements of Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and Section 474 b(5) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 for complete and proper documentation of every claim against government funds; and (ii) together with the Municipal Engineer, submit the lacking documents mentioned above; and
The Municipal Engineer (i) ensure that reports generated from his office are prepared properly and accurately to avoid misleading the users of the reports; and (ii) be guided by Annex E of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Memorandum No. 2005-027 dated February 27, 2005 in the grant of time extension and COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 in the information and publicity on programs/projects/activities of the Municipality.

observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Inventory Committee conducted a partial physical count of properties for CY 2020 and submitted its report thereon to the Office of the Auditor on April 21, 2021.
On-going updating of the PPELCs and PCs is being facilitated by the Municipal Accounting Office and the Municipal General Services Office, respectively.
observations.reason: Due to the pandemic, manpower is limited, thus complete physical count of the Municipality’s PPE and full implementation of the audit recommendation was not facilitated.

observations.managementAction: In compliance with Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011, the OIC – Municipal Accountant prepared and submitted Bank Reconciliation Statements for all the bank accounts maintained by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances totaling P4,249,638.60 and direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to send final demand letters to persons who are out of government service and consider the possibility of recovering the amount from any unclaimed benefits. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer to exhaust the legal remedies provided under COA Circular No. 2012-004 to enforce settlement of the long outstanding cash advances.

No action was taken by Management to fully comply with the audit recommendation. Further, the BAC did not observe the appropriate rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in the Trust Fund books to reconcile the balance of the Trust Liability - DRRMF presented in the Statement of Financial Position with the disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Accountant reconciled the balance of the Trust Liability – DRRMF presented in the Statement of Financial Position. However, no disclosure was provided on the unutilized balance of the LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the disclosure requirements under IPSAS 1 and COA Circular No. 2015-008 and designate a personnel to assist her in the preparation of complete and adequate Notes to Financial Statements and all the required financial information thereon to help the users in understanding the financial statements and to provide them with additional relevant information on the affected accounts.
observations.managementAction: On-going gathering of pertinent information and other details for the establishment of the GAD database is being facilitated by the concerned employees and officials.
observations.reason: Due to the pandemic, manpower is limited, thus implementation of the audit recommendation was delayed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to take action on the immediate remittance of excess funds from the completed projects involving a total amount of P724,717.24 to the Source Agency pursuant to Section 122 of the GAAM, Volume 1 and COA Circular 94-013.
observations.managementAction: Of the P724,717.24, the total amount of P567,862.24 was remitted to the source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury, to where it was applicable. As for P156,855.00 from the Department of Agriculture (DA) for the implementation of the integrated community food production, the excess fund shall be reprogrammed for the implementation of other Program, Projects and Activities (PPAs) as advised by the DA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider providing technical and financial assistance in the establishment of fully operational MRF with composting facilities for barangays or clusters of barangays, and proper maintenance thereof to address the solid waste management problems in the Municipality pursuant to Section 44 of Republic Act No. 9003 and Section 33 of RA No. 7160. We also recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board monitor and regularly inspect the condition and operation of established Barangay Material Recovery Facilities as required under Section 4(b) and (c), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor directed the concerned officials to find a conducive location for the establishment of the fully operational MRF. In addition, acquisition of land for the establishment of a fully operational MRF will be included in the CY 2022 budget. Regular inspection of the condition and operation of the established Barangay MRF are being conducted pursuant to Section 4(b) and (c), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.
observations.reason: Due to the pandemic, implementation of the audit recommendation was delayed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Person and all other concerned GAD officials to prepare the GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report using the format prescribed in Annexes D and E of the JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA and submit the same to the Provincial Planning Development Office and DILG Provincial Office for review.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person and all other concerned officials prepared the GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report using the format prescribed in Annexes D and E of the JMC No. 2013-01 and submitted the same as required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Executive Committee and GAD Technical Working Group to provide technical assistance to the Local Planning and Development Office (LPDO) to establish and maintain the GAD Database and ensure that it is operational. Likewise, allocate sufficient funds for its establishment.
observations.managementAction: On-going gathering of pertinent information and other details for the establishment of the GAD database is being facilitated by the concerned employees and officials.
observations.reason: Due to the pandemic, manpower is limited, thus implementation of the audit recommendation was delayed.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterate our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:

through the MENRO or SWMB and Liga ng mga Barangay encourage active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the Municipality by educating their respective constituents through intensive Information Education Campaign (IEC) on (i) the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes; (ii) prohibition on open burning; (iii) the importance of their participation in the implementation of various SWM programs; and (iv) strict implementation of “no segregation, no collection” policy; and

through the SWMB, (i) consider that garbage collectors and concerned personnel handling the collected waste of the municipality are properly trained and customize the truck used in the collection of waste to contain appropriate compartments to efficiently sort and store the waste while in transit; and (ii) fast track the processing of various requirements for the operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41 of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time to avert adverse environmental consequences.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, the following were done; (a) in December, garbage collectors and other personnel of the Municipality were properly trained how to handle solid waste through a seminar given by the Health Office; (b) garbage bins were procured wherein garbage/ wastes were segregated by class and hauled in the garbage truck instead of putting compartment in the garbage truck; (c) Information Education Campaign (IEC) thru house to house campaign and assemblies informing constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes and the importance of their participation in the implementation of various Solid Waste Management (SWM) programs were  conducted by the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB); and (d)	open burning of waste was strictly prohibited in the Municipality.
observations.reason: The Sanitary Landfill is not yet established by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

Inventory Committee to (i) conduct the physical count of inventories and property, plant and equipment (PPEs) in accordance with Section 124 of Volume I of Manual on NGAS for LGUs and submit the RPCI and RCPPE to the Audit Team within the timeframe provided by the same regulation; and (ii) prepare the RPCI and RPCPPE accurately by accomplishing all the required data such as the columns for article, unit of measure and shortage/overage on quantity and value and the remarks portion specifically the conditions of the inventories and PPEs at time of the physical count;

Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant to (i) exert efforts in tracing the undocumented office supplies inventories in the SEF amounting to P5,000.00; and (ii) always reconcile the result of the count with the property records and accounting records; and

Municipal Treasurer to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the IIRUP which could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, the RPCI and RPCPPE were already approved by the Municipal Mayor being the head of the agency.  Moreover, the inventories under “Food Supplies for Distribution” totaling P69,903.00 were already adjusted as expense under JEV Nos. 100-2004-47B and 100-2006-81B dated April 28, 2020 and June 29, 2020, respectively.
observations.reason: The Municipality incurred lapses in the inventory taking and recording of inventories and property, plant and equipment (PPE), as follows: (a) the Inventory Committee failed to submit the complete RPCPPE; (b) incurred lapses in accomplishing the RPCI and RPCPPE; (c) failed to classify the properties into proper accounts; and (d) failed to prepare and submit the Inventory and Inspection of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) strictly observe the existing rules and regulations governing collections and deposits of government funds; (b) together with all the collecting officers, strengthen the internal control in cash management by observing the proper cut-off in the reporting of collections, particularly the preparation of Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD); (c) instruct all the collecting officers to (i) ensure completeness of information on the RCD such as signatures and dates; (ii) prepare and maintain the individual cash book to record the collections and remittances; and (iii) prepare and submit the monthly Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) for the months of November and December 2019; (d) prepare and submit the monthly Consolidated Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) as mentioned above pursuant to Section 37 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2020, collections are deposited intact.  Also, cash books were already prepared and maintained by the collecting officers.  And, monthly RAAF and CRAAF were already prepared and submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: It was still observed that the Municipal Treasurer’s Office failed to establish proper cut off in the reporting of collections, particularly the preparation of RCDs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the (a) LDRRMO (i) submit the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM to the Audit Team in accordance with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and (ii) consider the requisitions of items needed to maintain pre-positioned food packs and hygiene kits for at least 500 families at any given time in compliance with the minimum standards and pursuant to NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1; and (b) Municipal Accountant strictly adhere with the guidelines set forth by (i) COA Circular No. 2012-002 for proper accounting and recording of the utilization of the LDRRMF and transfer of unexpended LDRRMF to Special Trust Fund; (ii) Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for proper documentation of transactions; and (iii) COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 for recording of transactions.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant (a) transferred the unexpended fund for Cash for Work in CY 2019 amounting to P1,306.00 to the Special Trust Fund (STF) on February 14, 2020; (b) submitted the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to the Audit Team; and
properly transferred the unexpended balance of Quick Response Fund (QRF) and Mitigation-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, lapses and deficiencies in the charging, recording and documentation of disbursements totaling P2,987,864.18  were still noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant and the concerned employee who prepares the payroll to strictly observe the provisions of Local Budget Circular No. 103.  The officer responsible in the computation of monthly RATA should always be guided by the daily time records, applications for leave of the department heads and attendance sheet of SB Members during sessions in order to come up with an accurate computation of RATA due; (b) Municipal Accountant to make sure that required supporting documents of payrolls are complete and accurate pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445; and (c) Department heads entitled to RATA to properly accomplish their DTRs and provide the required supporting documents to avoid confusion as to the nature of the absences.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) cause the refund of the net excess payments of RATA amounting to P11,200.00 from the officials and employees of the Municipality who were paid more than what is due to them; and (b) consider the timing of the payments of RATA to avoid the incorrect payment due to incomplete supporting documents and lack of basis of the number of actual work performance.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2020, the net excess payment of RATA for CY 2019 amounting to P11,200.00 were refunded by the concerned officials and employees.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, excess and under payments of RATA amounting P4,050.00 and P2,025.00, respectively, were still observed.  Moreover, payments were still not supported with complete and proper documentation and the absences in the DTRs were left blank without any remarks and not supported with valid documents to identify the nature of the absences. We have issued Audit Observation No. 2021-006(1) dated April 22, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) require strict implementation of the guidelines in the liquidations of cash advances; (b) see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers and liquidation reports are accurate and complete before processing the claims; and (c) be guided by the Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 on the recording of transactions of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Certificates of Appearance and Certifications of Expenses not requiring receipt for expenses amounting to P300.00 and below were already attached in the liquidations and the payments for registration fees were already recorded as Training Expenses.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, there were liquidations wherein copies/ photocopies of the official receipts for the refund of excess cash advance were not attached to the liquidation reports and some agency personnel still claimed fares for taxi, grab and van from temporary residence to the venue of seminars totaling P2,443.50  which are already covered by incidental expenses.  We have issued Audit Observation No. 2021-006(3) dated April 22, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

The Municipal Mayor along with all the concerned officials and employees strictly follow the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 in planning the programs/projects/activities funded by 20% DF and their implementation.  See to it that those development projects identified in the AIP were fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents;

The Municipal Accountant (i) be guided by the requirements of Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160 for the complete and proper documentation of every claim against government funds and the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 in recording the transactions of the Municipality; and (ii) submit the lacking documents mentioned above; and

The Municipal Engineer ensure that reports generated from his office are prepared properly and accurately to avoid misleading the users of the reports.
observations.managementAction: As-built plans, letter from the contractor for the release of the payments, and Contractor's All Risk Insurance (CARI) were already attached in the payments of infrastructure projects.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, the Municipality failed to implement 10 projects funded by the current and continuing appropriations of the 20% Development Fund (DF) with a total budget amount of P5,934,539.07 due to failure to properly schedule the implementation of projects; and still incurred deficiencies and lapses in the documentation of transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the (a) Head of GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) Technical Working Group (TWG) make it sure that GAD Accomplishment Report and its supporting documents are prepared properly and complete in accordance with the provisions of Section 4.1.C.8 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 as amended by Section 7.0 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01; (b) GFPS provide technical assistance in the implementation of the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and monitor its implementation so that optimal utilization of the fund could be achieved for the benefits of the intended beneficiaries pursuant to Section 6.0 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01; and (c) Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, (i) see to it that supporting documents attached to disbursement vouchers are accurate and complete before processing claims pursuant to Section 474 b (5) of R.A. No. 7160 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445; and (ii) be guided by the provisions of EO No. 77 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 in the claims for travel and for recording of transactions, respectively.
observations.managementAction: Cash advances charged to the fund were already supported with approved payroll or lists of payees indicating their net payments and cash advances for cash for work were already recorded in the Advances to Special Disbursing Officer account.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, infractions were still noted in the preparation and submission of the Accomplishment Report and their supporting documents and lapses and deficiencies were still incurred in the documentation and recording of disbursements.
