observations.recommendation: 1.7 	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Municipal Accountant to (a) analyze, review, monitor all dormant asset accounts, to ensure that (i) only substantiated accounts are recognized in the books; and (ii) dormant, long outstanding and unsubstantiated accounts are derecognized accordingly; (b) prepare a formal request to the COA through the Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) for the authority to write-off the dormant and  substantiated receivable accounts; and (c) provide an allowance for impairment on receivable accounts based on analysis of collection rating and collectability of the accounts to ensure the fairness of accounts presentation in the financial statements.

2.13	We recommended that the LCE (a) require the Municipal Treasurer,  also the General Services Officer, to  prioritize the conduct of physical inventory of PPE and reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records; (b) require the Accounting Office and Treasury Office/GSO to maintain the appropriate property records such as PPELC and PLC, respectively, and provide the necessary allowance for depreciation on all depreciable assets of the Municipality; (c) instruct the Municipal officials concerned to exert effort to facilitate the transfer of the title of the purchased parcel of land in the name of the Municipality, the soonest possible time; and (d) immediately facilitate the disposal of unserviceable property to prevent further loss and deterioration.

3.5 	We commended the efforts made by Management in efficiently and effectively collecting their tax revenues. We further recommended that the Municipality continuously conduct tax campaigns to increase its revenue collections and source out funds for programs, projects and activities that would   further improve the delivery of basic services to its constituents.

4.14 	 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) direct the concerned officials to observe strictly the general guidelines on the information and publicity of projects undertaken by the Municipality in accordance with the COA Circular No. 2013-004  dated January 30, 2013; (b) require the  Municipal Engineer to (i) ensure that the infrastructure projects conform with the documentary requirements provided by the DPWH Standard Specifications for Highways, Bridges and Airport; (ii) require the contractors to  repair the defective and damaged portion of the road projects pursuant to the one year warranty period  from the date of project completion up to final acceptance or the defects liability period pursuant to Section 62 of the CY 2016 IRR of RA No. 9184; (c) require the Municipal Planning Officer and the Municipal Engineer to implement and monitor regularly the status of all implemented projects as embodied in their local development plan; and (d) require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the documentary requirements for infrastructure projects prior to payment.

5.7	We recommended that the LCE require the concerned officials to (a) prepare the LDRRMF Utilization Report regularly on a monthly basis; and (b) refrain from using the DRRMF for transactions which are not calamity and/or disaster-related.

6.5	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the MENRO (a) involve closely the Barangay officials in solid waste management and activate the Solid Waste Management Committees in every Barangay for the strict enforcement and continuous implementation of the existing environmental laws especially on establishment of MRF and waste segregation at source; (b) intensify information campaign on solid waste management and educate the constituents on the necessity of   the construction of MRF in every Barangay and establishing programs on composting, recycling, re-use, and recovery of solid waste generated; (c) consider extending financial assistance, resources, and technology to all of the three Barangays which  remained non-compliant to the basic requirements of R.A. No. 9003; and  (d)develop programs on  composting, recycling, re-use, and recovery of solid waste generated in order to reduce hauling and disposal costs of the Municipality.

7.6	We recommended that Management (a) require the MSWDO to strictly allocate not more than 1% of the Internal Revenue Allotment for Local Council for Protection of Children so as not to deprive other programs, projects and activities (PPAs) of the Municipality of needed funds; and (b) direct the MSWDO to execute the PPAs, monitor their implementation, and  provide documented reports on the impact of the PPAs in order to address the various issues and concerns pertaining to the children’s welfare.

8.7	We recommended that Management (a) require the Municipal Budget Officer together with the focal person and focal departments/offices to continue appropriating at least 5% for GAD-related activities in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01: Guidelines on the Localization of Magna Carta of Women and (b) require the focal persons to follow up from the concerned authorized focal departments the timely submission of accomplishment report, make a clear assessment of the GAD PPAs for monitoring  and evaluation of the GAD implementation, and submit to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report within five working days from end of January of the ensuing year.
9.3	We recommended that Management continue to faithfully comply with BIR RMC No. 23-2012 dated February 14, 2012 on the withholding and remittance of taxes within the prescribed period.
10.5	We recommended that Management continue to comply with regulations on the deduction and remittance of premiums and loan amortizations including the government share within the prescribed period.
11.4	We recommended that Management continue to comply with regulations on the withholding and remittance of premiums and loan amortizations within the prescribed period.
12.4	We recommended that Management continue to comply with regulations on the withholding and remittance of monthly PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the (a) Management   instruct the (i) officials involved in the processing of claims to be more diligent in evaluating supporting papers of claims to avoid suspensions and disallowances in audit; and (ii)  responsible and liable persons require the immediate refund of disallowed claims once these have become final and executory; and (b) Municipal Accountant to comply with the revised documentary requirements for common government transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to establish the regularity of transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
➢ Municipal Inventory Committee to conduct physical count for all the property
owned by the Municipality and submit the Report on the Physical Count of PPE
within the reglementary period as required under Section 124 of the MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume I;
➢ Municipal General Services Officer to reconcile the physical inventory report
with the PPE ledger cards of the Municipal Accounting Office;
➢ OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer to continue
updating their Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELCs) and
Property Cards (PCs), respectively, and provide the complete and necessary
information in both records; and
➢ OIC-Municipal Accountant to draw necessary adjusting entries to reflect the
correct PPE account balances in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the material variance of P15,043,340.92
between their records and effect the necessary adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant
to draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record all valid reconciling items in the
books of account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-Municipal
Accountant to prepare the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements for the three
bank accounts and record all reconciling items accordingly in compliance with
Section 74 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the immediate liquidation of
the outstanding cash advances totaling P4,249,638.60 and direct the OIC-
Municipal Accountant to send final demand letters to persons who are out of
government service and consider the possibility of recovering the amount from
any unclaimed benefits.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer
to exhaust the legal remedies provided under COA Circular No. 2012-004 to
enforce the settlement of the long outstanding cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant
to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in the Trust Fund books to reconcile the
balance of the Trust Liability - DRRMF presented in the Statement of Financial
Position with the disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements by debiting
Due from Other Funds and crediting Trust Liability – DRRMF both by
P4,755,341.78.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant
to classify and present the current portion of the long-term loans payable in the
amount of P697,228.89 as part of the current liabilities and the remaining
amount which is due for settlement beyond one year as non-current liabilities in
the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant
to strictly comply with the disclosure requirements under IPSAS 1 and COA
Circular No. 2015-008 and designate a personnel to assist her in the preparation
of complete and adequate Notes to Financial Statements and all the required
financial information thereon to help the users in understanding the financial
statements and to provide them with additional relevant information on the
affected accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advice the OIC-Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to take action on the immediate
remittance of excess funds from completed projects involving a total amount of
P724,717.24 to the Source Agency pursuant to Section 122 of the GAAM,
Volume 1 and COA Circular 94-013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to issue
check directly to the suppliers/dealers and withhold taxes provided under relevant
tax laws and regulations issued by the BIR.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards
Committee to observe appropriate procurement procedure provided under RA
No. 9184 for procurement of goods and services.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor request the Sangguniang Bayan to
formulate policies and guidelines to govern the grant of financial assistance to
the qualified constituents, which includes but is not limited to the eligibility of
the beneficiaries, the limitation on the amount to be given in every particular
case and the case/s to be given priority and the all requirements needed.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Management
Information Systems Office to create a data bank of all the Municipality
indigents for ready reference in processing financial assistance and other
related transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:


     a.  Inventory  Committee  to  (i)  conduct  the  physical   count   of
        inventories and property, plant and equipment (PPEs) in  accordance
        with Section 124 of Volume I of Manual on NGAS for LGUs and  submit
        the RPCI and RCPPE to the Audit Team within the timeframe  provided
        by the same regulation; (ii) prepare the RPCI and RPCPPE accurately
        by accomplishing all the required data  such  as  the  columns  for
        article, unit of measure and shortage/overage on quantity and value
        and  the  remarks  portion  specifically  the  conditions  of   the
        inventories and PPEs at time of the physical count;


     b. Inventory Committee and  the  Municipal  Accountant  to  (i)  exert
        efforts in tracing the undocumented office supplies inventories  in
        the SEF amounting to  P5,000.00;  and  (ii)  always  reconcile  the
        result of the  count  with  the  property  records  and  accounting
        records; and


     c.  Municipal  Treasurer  to  make  a  report  of   unserviceable/non-
        functional  PPEs  in  the   IIRUP   which   could   be   undertaken
        simultaneously  with  the  physical  inventory  for  disposal   and
        eventual proper relief of accountabilities.

We recommended that the  Municipal  Mayor  instruct  the  Municipal
   Accountant, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Treasurer,  Municipal  Assessor
   and other personnel concerned to submit the copies of the  abovementioned
   required documents to the Audit  Team  to  establish  the  propriety  and
   validity of the above transactions.

Based on  the  foregoing  observations,  we  recommended  that  the
   Municipal  Treasurer  (a)  strictly  observe  the  existing   rules   and
   regulations governing collections and deposits of government  funds;  (b)
   together with  all  the  collecting  officers,  strengthen  the  internal
   control in cash  management  by  observing  the  proper  cut-off  in  the
   reporting of collections,  particularly  the  preparation  of  Report  of
   Collections and Deposits (RCD); (c) instruct all the collecting  officers
   to (i) ensure completeness of information on the RCD such  as  signatures
   and dates; (ii) prepare and maintain the individual cash book  to  record
   the collections and remittances; and (iii) prepare and submit the monthly
   Report on Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) for the  months  of
   November  and  December  2019;  (d)  prepare  and  submit   the   monthly
   Consolidated Report on Accountability for Accountable  Forms  (CRAAF)  as
   mentioned above pursuant to Section 37 of COA Circular No.  92-382  dated
   July 3, 1992.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended  that  the  (a)
   LDRRMO (i) submit the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization  of  DRRM
   to the Audit Team in accordance with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-
   002 dated September 12, 2012; and (ii) consider the requisitions of items
   needed to maintain pre-positioned food packs  and  hygiene  kits  for  at
   least 500 families at any given  time  in  compliance  with  the  minimum
   standards and  pursuant  to  NDRRMC-DBM-DILG  JMC  No.  2013-1;  and (b)
   Municipal Accountant strictly adhere with the guidelines set forth by (i)
   COA Circular No. 2012-002 for proper  accounting  and  recording  of  the
   utilization of the LDRRMF and transfer of unexpended  LDRRMF  to  Special
   Trust Fund; (ii) Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular  No.  2012-
   001 for proper documentation of transactions; and (iii) COA Circular  No.
   2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 for recording of transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the (a)  Municipal
   Accountant and  the  concerned  employee  who  prepares  the  payroll  to
   strictly observe the provisions of Local Budget Circular  No.  103.   The
   officer responsible in the computation of monthly RATA should  always  be
   guided  by  the  daily  time  records,  applications  for  leave  of  the
   department heads and attendance sheet of SB Members  during  sessions  in
   order to come up with an accurate computation of RATA due; (b)  Municipal
   Accountant to make sure that required supporting  documents  of  payrolls
   are complete and accurate pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD  No.  1445;  and
   (c) Department heads entitled to RATA to properly accomplish  their  DTRs
   and provide the required supporting documents to avoid  confusion  as  to
   the nature of the absences.


        We further recommended that  the  Municipal  Mayor  (a)  cause  the
   refund of the net excess payments of RATA amounting  to  P11,200.00  from
   the officials and employees of the Municipality who were paid  more  than
   what is due to them; and (b) consider the timing of the payments of  RATA
   to avoid the incorrect payment due to incomplete supporting documents and
   lack of basis of the number of actual work performance.

We recommended that the Municipal  Accountant  (a)  require  strict
   implementation of the guidelines in the liquidations  of  cash  advances;
   (b) see to it that supporting documents  attached  to  the  vouchers  and
   liquidation reports are  accurate  and  complete  before  processing  the
   claims; and (c) be guided by the Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009  on
   the recording of transactions of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management direct the MTO and Accounting offices to (a) maintain   Property Cards in the MTO office   for easy reconciliation of the PPE account; (b) exert efforts to secure the land title for the untitled lot purchased by the Municipality; (c) immediately request for disposal of unserviceable property to prevent their further deterioration;(d) disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the  carrying amount of  property, plant and equipment retired from active use and held for disposal so as not to mislead users of the Financial Statements; and  (e) prepare the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) to correct the erroneous charging of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Municipal Treasurer has been instructed to maintain the Property Cards and work on the titling of the lot purchased. The documents for the disposal of the unserviceable properties were in process. The JEV for the correction of the erroneous charging of accounts was already prepared by the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (a) coordinate with the Office of the Provincial Accountant to request a monthly report on the receivables of the Municipality to serve as basis in recording the Due from LGUs – PGB and the corresponding Tax Revenue accounts; and (b) request the timely remittance of the share of the Municipality on the Real Property Tax, Tax on Sand, Gravel and Other Quarry Products, and Amusement Tax collected by the PGB.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer coordinated with the Provincial Accountant regarding the said report. In addition, they requested for the timely remittance of the municipal share on the said collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (a) remit to the source agencies or to the Bureau of the Treasury the unexpended balance of the trust funds totaling to ₱305,455.27;  and (b) recognize in the books the liquidation of the Aid from Barangay totaling to  ₱239,000.00 representing  its share in  the purchase of a motor vehicle.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it  returned the amount of ₱167,603.85 as per check number 690269 and that it  recorded the liquidation of the Aid from Barangay totaling ₱239,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (a) strictly observe the standard procurement procedure and attach all the required documentary requirements to establish the validity and occurrence of government transactions; and (b) ensure that all payments must be made by check issued directly under the name of the supplier.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will observe the standard procedure and will  attach the necessary documents for government transactions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management consolidate all purchases and strictly adopt competitive bidding as the general method of procurement pursuant to the provisions of R.A. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will coordinate with the  Deped  District  to properly plan its procurement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the District Supervisors of  Central, West and East District to (a)  submit written report to justify/explain the necessity of disbursing ₱231,350.40 for food during the EDDIS IV athletic meet; and  (b) Consolidate the disbursement vouchers and payrolls for the EDDIS IV Athletic Meet and Provincial Athletic Meet and attach the supporting documents  such as invoices, official receipts and payrolls to support the liquidation of all  the disbursement of funds spent for sports development.
observations.managementAction: The District Supervisors explained that the food disbursed out of the SEF were for the athletes. The food and other expenses of the officiating officials were charged to the funds collected from the share of each District for the athletic meet.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) instruct the MDC and key officials to continue to adhere strictly to the guidelines on  project identification and inclusion in the AIP of development projects and its utilization pursuant  to DILG and DBM  JMC No. 2017-1; (b) require the  Municipal Engineer to  i) ensure that the  infrastructure projects conform with the documentary requirements provided by the DPWH Standard Specifications for Highways, Bridges and Airport, ii)  respective contractors to  repair the defective and damaged portion of the road projects pursuant to  the one year warranty from the date of project completion up to final acceptance or the defects liability period pursuant to Section 62 of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184; c) professionalize the BAC Secretariat and TWG in the performance of their mandated functions and responsibilities specially  in complying with PhilGEPS requirements; and d) require the  Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the documentary requirements for infrastructure projects  before payment is made.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will adhere with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the (a) concerned officials to refrain from using the DRRMF for transactions which are not disaster-related; and (b) Municipal Accountant to i)recognize in the books the procured equipment as Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment account; and ii)prepare the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) to correct the erroneous charging of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will correct the erroneously charged accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the MENRO (a) involve closely the barangay officials in solid waste management and activate the Solid Waste Management Committees in every barangay for the strict enforcement and continuous implementation of the existing environmental laws especially on waste segregation at source; (b) intensify information campaign on solid waste management and educate the constituents on the necessity of   the construction of MRF in every barangay and establishing programs on composting, recycling, re-use, and recovery of solid waste generated; (c) consider extending financial assistance, resources, and technology to all of the six Barangays which  remained non-compliant to the basic requirements of R.A. No. 9003; and (d) install control measures in regulating wastes collection at source to final disposal to ensure correctness of amounts paid for wastes disposal.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will comply with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management (a) through theGFPS coordinate with the Planning Department in the completion of GAD database; and (b) through the GAD Focal Point Person ensure the submission of the annual GPB for review and approval of the DILG in conformity with the relevant provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will coordinate the completion of the GAD database and the timely preparation of the annual GPB.

observations.recommendation: We commended the efforts made by Management in the efficient and effective collection of their tax revenues. We further recommended that the Municipality continuously conduct tax campaigns to further improve the delivery of basic services to its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will ensure the continuous efficient and effective collection of its tax revenues.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue to faithfully comply with BIR RMC No. 23-2012 on the withholding and remittance of taxes within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will continue to comply with BIR RMC No. 23-2012 on the withholding and remittance of taxes within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue to comply with regulations on the deduction and remittance of premiums and loan amortizations including the government share within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will continue to comply with regulations on the deduction and remittance of premiums and loan amortizations including the government share within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue to comply with regulations on the withholding and remittance of premiums and loan amortizations within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will continue to comply with regulations on the withholding and remittance of premiums and loan amortizations within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management continue to comply with regulations on the withholding and remittance of monthly contributions within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that it will continue to comply with regulations on the withholding and remittance of monthly contributions within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:

require the Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts in tracing the undocumented PPEs. Henceforth, compliance with Sections 119, 120 and 123, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs and Section 375(d) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160 should be strictly observed; and

require the Municipal Treasurer to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the IIRUP which could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2019, properties labeled as undocumented in the last year RPCPPE were already identified and broken-down into various properties with descriptions. Also, the property and stock numbers of each property, plant and equipment (PPE) and inventories were already reflected in the RPCPPE and RPCI, respectively.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee still failed to properly accomplish the columns for article, unit of measure and shortage/overage on quantity and value of the RPCI and RPCPPE. Also the conditions of the inventories and properties were not reflected in the inventory reports.    In addition, there was still inventories amounting to P5,000.00 under Office Supplies Inventory of Special Education Fund labeled as undocumented.  And the IIRUP was not yet submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) ensure a) consistency of the PPAs and appropriated/allocated amount incorporated in the LDRRMFIP and AIP; and b) that projects and activities in the LDRRMFIP be integrated in the Municipality’s AIP; and

The Municipal Accountant be guided with COA Circular No. 2015-009 which prescribes the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Unit (LGUs) and COA Circular No. 2014-002 which provides the guidelines on the receipt and utilization DRRMF in recording the transactions of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality already included in its CY 2019 LDRRMFIP the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years and the projects, programs and activities and their appropriated/allocated amount incorporated in the LDRRMFIP and Annual Investment Plan were already consistent.
observations.reason: Lapses in recording of transactions for CY 2019 were still noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

through the Liga ng mga Barangay encourage active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the Municipality by educating their respective constituents through Information Education Campaign (IEC) on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes and the importance of their participation  in the implementation of various SWM programs;

through the MENRO or SWMB, comply with the requirements of R.A. No. 9003 on waste segregation by: (a) placing trash bins with proper labels on the designated garbage collection area and other public places and collect only those wastes which are properly segregated; and (b) ensuring that the existing MRF in Barangays are functional and the purpose of the establishment of MRF is being served.

through the Sangguniang Bayan (SB), cause the preparation and issuance of the IRR for proper implementation of Municipal Ordinance No. 01-96 Series of 2011; and

through the SWMB, fast track the processing of various requirements for the operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41 of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time to avert adverse environmental consequences.
observations.reason:
Same audit observations noted in prior years such as (a) non-implementation of mandatory waste segregation at source; (b) open burning of solid waste; and (c) no final disposal facility or sanitary landfill were still not fully addressed by the Municipality. 
Audit Observation (AOM) No. 2020-010 dated June 23, 2020 was issued to the agency.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The Municipal Accountant a) remind the employees concerned to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period as provided under Section 3.1.2.1 of COA Circular No. 96-004; and b) ensure that the Itineraries of Travel and Certificate of Travel Completed (Appendix B) are properly accomplished and travel orders are attached to the liquidation reports pursuant to COA Circular 2012-001; and

The Municipal Mayor secure written approval from the Governor for any official travel outside the province in accordance with Section 96 of R.A. No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2019, Itineraries of Travel and Certificates of Travel Completed were already   properly accomplished.  Likewise, travel orders were already attached on the Liquidation Reports.  And claims for travel outside the province of the Municipal Mayor were already supported with authority from the Governor.
observations.reason: Full liquidation of cash advances thru payroll deduction or deduction to the next claims for travel were still observed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The Municipal Mayor along with all the concerned officials and employees strictly follow the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 in planning the programs/projects/activities funded by 20% DF and their implementation. See to it that those development projects identified in the AIP were fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents. As to the lack of manpower issue, it is recommended that the LGU fast track the hiring of a full fledge Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to unburden the Municipal Engineer of the duties and responsibilities brought by his designation as OIC – MPDO;

The MPDC ensure that the programs, projects and activities chargeable under the 20% Development Fund partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes; and

The Municipal Accountant be guided by the requirements of Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160 in the complete and proper documentation of every claim against government funds and the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 in recording the transactions of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: Savings and balances from the 21 non-moving/unimplemented projects from CYs 2015 to 2018 were reprogrammed thru Municipal Development Council Resolution No. 03-2019.  Moreover, eight out of 10 projects funded by CY 2019 20% DF were implemented during the year.
observations.reason: Three projects remained unimplemented as of December 31, 2019.  Moreover, lapses and deficiencies in the documentation and recording of transactions were still noted.

observations.managementAction: The GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) for CY 2019 was reviewed and endorsed by the DILG Provincial Office.    Allocation of projects and programs per GPB, AIP and Budget were already consistent.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2019, one organization-focused program and two attributed programs embodied in the GPB were not implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management take immediate steps to dispose of all unserviceable properties to prevent possible loss and avoid further deterioration in accordance with the guidelines provided by the COA Circular No. 89-296 dated January 27, 1989.
observations.reason: Management is still preparing the documents needed for the disposal of the unserviceable properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management instruct the GAD Focal Point System to (a) coordinate with the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) and fast track the updating of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; (b) to submit all the reports required under MCW; and (c) utilize the fund in accordance with the guidelines on the matter.

We recommended that Management direct the MTO and Accounting offices to (a) maintain   Property Cards in the MTO office   for easy reconciliation of the PPE account; (b) exert efforts to secure the land title for the untitled lot purchased by the Municipality; (c) immediately request for disposal of unserviceable property to prevent their further deterioration;(d) disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the  carrying amount of  property, plant and equipment retired from active use and held for disposal so as not to mislead users of the Financial Statements; and  (e) prepare the corresponding Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) to correct the erroneous charging of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (a) coordinate with the Office of the Provincial Accountant to request a monthly report on the receivables of the Municipality to serve as basis in recording the Due from LGUs – PGB and the corresponding Tax Revenue accounts; and (b) request the timely remittance of the share of the Municipality on the Real Property Tax, Tax on Sand, Gravel and Other Quarry Products, and Amusement Tax collected by the PGB.

We recommended that Management (a) remit to the source agencies or to the Bureau of the Treasury the unexpended balance of the trust funds totaling to ₱305,455.27;  and (b) recognize in the books the liquidation of the Aid from Barangay totaling to  ₱239,000.00 representing  its share in  the purchase of a motor vehicle.

We recommended that Management (a) strictly observe the standard procurement procedure and attach all the  required documentary requirements to establish the validity and occurrence of government transactions; and (b) ensure that all payments must be made by check issued directly under the name of the supplier.
We recommended that Management consolidate all purchases and strictly adopt competitive bidding as the general method of procurement pursuant to the provisions of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that Management direct the District Supervisors of  Central, West and East District to (a)  submit written report to justify/explain the necessity of disbursing ₱231,350.40 for food during the EDDIS IV athletic meet; and  (b) Consolidate the disbursement vouchers and payrolls for the EDDIS IV Athletic Meet and Provincial Athletic Meet and attach the supporting documents  such as invoices, official receipts and payrolls to support the liquidation of all  the disbursement of funds spent for sports development.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the  Municipal Accountant   update and  prepare regularly  the bank reconciliation statements at the end of each month for the five  bank accounts of the municipality to ensure accuracy of the Cash account in compliance with Sections  3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-111.

We recommend the following:
That management complies strictly with the above-cited rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
That cash advances be liquidated immediately after utilization. Also, the proper recording of cash advances should be strictly implemented.
That management exert extra effort to demand the liquidation of the above advances and if not utilized to demand the refund of the amount.

We recommended that the Honorable Chief Executive to instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to submit the duly cancelled staled checks for the verification with its original disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) comply strictly with the provision of Sec. 48.2 of the Government Procurement Reform Act of 2003 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations.  Splitting of contracts is not allowed.  The alternative method of procurement for shopping or small value procurement be adopted only whenever justified by the conditions and within the threshold provided by the procurement law.
We recommended that the General Service Officer requires the submission of duly accomplished daily trip tickets before the use of government vehicles.
For the above observations, we recommend the submission of any legal basis/proof for the disbursements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  require the preparation of an Annual Procurement Plan and Project Procurement Management Plan in compliance with the provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184.
We recommended that the concerned office submit the required documents as mandated by  COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.
We recommended that the concerned LGU personnel submit the required reports on time as mandated by Republic Act 101211 dated May 27, 2010.

We recommended that management require all persons liable to immediately settle the suspensions and disallowances of P44,920,939.96. Settlement of  the  audit suspensions is through compliance with the requirements indicated for our evaluation. Items suspended in audit which are not settled within ninety (90) days from receipt shall become a disallowance pursuant to Section 82 of P.D. No. 1445.Audit Disallowances not appealed within 6 months becomes final and executory.

1.9   We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the Inventory Committee to complete the physical count of all PPE and submit the report on or before January 31 of the following year; (b) consider the  appointment  of a General Services Officer (GSO) who will handle the receipt, issuance and  recording of the procured and issued  property; (c) require the Acting General Service Officer to maintain Property Cards  and  the Municipal Accountant to  maintain Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards   for easy reconciliation of records; (d)   require the Inventory  Committee  to  investigate  the non-existing  Books and Work/Zoo Animals  accounts and if evidences warrant, derecognize  them from the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Municipal Accountant to  analyze, review, monitor all  dormant asset accounts  and adjust the books to ensure that only valid accounts  are recorded; and (b)  prepare a formal request to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising Auditor (SA) for  the derecognition and/or adjustment in the books of accounts of all  dormant, long outstanding and unsubstantiated accounts.

3.6   We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the Municipal Accountant to observe the perpetual inventory method of accounting for inventories pursuant to Section 114 of NGAS Manual, Volume I; (b) require all Department Heads/requisitioning units to prepare the  RIS for every request for supplies issuances for submission to the Municipal Treasurer/ Acting  GSO ; and (c) require the Supply Officer  to prepare the monthly MSSMI duly supported by RIS from end-users for submission to the Accounting Office for proper recording of issuances of inventories.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to:  (a) submit to the COA Auditor’s Office the advance copies of the duly signed contracts/purchase orders within five days upon its perfection in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009; and (b) provide copies of the approved contracts/purchase orders to the Municipal Treasurer/Acting GSO and the Supply Officer for monitoring of delivery and inspection.
5.8    We recommended that the Municipality of Santa Maria adhere to the provisions contained in the existing MOA dated April 26, 2011 unless a revised approved MOA is in place.

6.5      We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to join efforts in determining the source of funds and cause the return of the idle, unused funds to the national government, and make the necessary adjustment in the books.

7.7   We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the regulations on the submission of accounts and financial reports in order to avoid delays in the verification and conduct of other audit-related activities; and (b) ensure that the required financial reports and documents are submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.

8.9        We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor  ensure that the procurement of medicines conform with existing government laws, rules and regulations and require (a) the requisitioning Municipal Health Officer to submit a certification that the        medicines being requisitioned fall within and conform with Executive Order No. 49, s 1993 and the Revised Implementing Guidelines for the Philippine National Formulary System; (b) the BAC to religiously post in PhilGEPS website all procurement opportunities, results of bidding and related information in the PhilGEPS bulletin board; and (c) the  Municipal Accountant  to submit the list of recipient-RHUs with acknowledgement on the receipt of medicines.

9.7      We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Local School Board and the Bids and Award Committee to submit an explanation on the procurement of the said textbook thereby incurring  irregular and unnecessary expenditures ; (b) require the CDS to submit an explanation on the disregard of authority from the Secretary of DepEd; (c) refrain from granting unauthorized requisitions of textbooks and other instructional materials   which are contrary to law, rules, regulations or issuances of the Secretary of DepEd; and (d) require the  DepEd  Central District Supervisor to submit the Acknowledgement Receipts from end-user schools of Central and East Districts to establish existence and completeness of the transaction.

10.5    We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and all department heads/end-user units to submit their PPMPs to the BAC as basis in the preparation of the APP. Strictly observe that no procurement shall be authorized and undertaken unless it is included in the APP.

11.6   We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the Inventory/Disposal Committee to dispose the unserviceable motor vehicle in accordance with existing rules and regulations in order to prevent further deterioration and generate additional funds for the Municipality; and (b) require the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the carrying amounts of the unserviceable property and after disposal, recognize the gain or loss arising from its derecognition.

12.19   We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) demand the immediate liquidation  of the  cash advance of Obando District Supervisor amounting to ₱300,000.00; (b) require the Municipal Accountant  to (i) stop the granting of cash advances to DepED officials and give cash advances only to designated Disbursing Officers  of the Municipality; (ii) desist from granting additional cash advances unless the previous cash advance has been liquidated; (c) require the Supply Officer  to  procure athletic expenses  through public bidding  paid by check and  the Municipal Accountant to observe full compliance with the  withholding tax law; (d) require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents attached to the transactions; (e) require Nuestra Señora del Carmen Transport Services Inc. to submit receipts with  ATPs conforming with BIR regulations; (f) require the three District Supervisors  to (i) submit project proposals and the  official list of participants for EDDIS Athletic Meet and KAB-O-REE; and (ii) refund the difference between the authorized and actual expenses incurred during the athletic meet amounting to ₱57,000.00; (g) require the concerned officials  to (i) submit the duly approved supporting documents for the benchmarking of IPEd in Tokyo, Japan and/or  refund the cash advance  due to the absence of students belonging to  Indigenous People in the Districts (ii) require the District Supervisors of District IV  to submit their authority to travel  from the National DepEd officials.

13.12    We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the BAC to adopt public bidding as the general mode of procurement and limit the use of alternative mode of procurement to highly exceptional cases only; (b) require the Municipal Accountant to observe proper recording of transactions in the books for fair presentation of asset and expense accounts; and (c) require the Supply Officer to prepare Inventory Custodian Slips (ICS) for the procurement of tangible items for monitoring and accountability purposes.

14.12    We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the Municipal Budget Officer to stop the practice of improperly charging inappropriate expenses to SEF which the DepEd has the mandate to disburse out of its own appropriation; (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from reimbursing expenses to DepEd District Supervisors which are not allowable charges to the fund; (c) require the refund of fuel reimbursement to a privately-owned vehicle with plate no. NM9666; (d) require the Supply Officer to prepare Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for the equipment issued to respective schools; (e) require the Municipal Local School Board to prepare a balanced budget observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on allocation and utilization of funds; and (f) require the Municipal Budget Officer to adhere strictly with  the approved budget and desist from incurring overdraft in appropriations.

15.9    We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a) Municipal Engineer to  i) ensure that the  infrastructure projects conform with the documentary requirements provided by the DPWH Standard Specifications for Highways, Bridges and Airport, ii)  respective contractors to  repair the defective and damaged portion of the road projects pursuant to  the one year warranty from the date of project completion up to final acceptance or the defects liability period pursuant to Section 62 of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184; and b)  Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the documentary requirements for infrastructure projects  before payment is made.

16.6   We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require  (a)  the Municipal Accountant to ensure that only expenditures which correspond to the identified gender issues and concerns    in the GAD  Plan  are charged against the  GAD Budget; (b) the GAD Focal Person to  (i) carefully identify and  include only PPAs that are gender responsive  in the GAD Plan and Budget to  address the  issues and concerns arising from the unequal status of women and men stakeholders, clients and constituents;  (ii )include in the annual budget  the allocation for GAD Fund for those PPAs  which are gender responsive and refrain from allocating for PPAs which are not GAD related; and (iii)  monitor the implementation of the GAD PPAs and prepare the accomplishment report  in the prescribed format.

17.18    We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) strictly comply with DENR regulations on the closure of the  open dump site by  preparing a new Rehabilitation Plan for the open dump site and provide a  budget  for its preparation; (b) evaluate the existing facility and equipment in order to identify those that need repair or maintenance services; (c) provide the necessary protective personal equipment to all wastes personnel directly involved in the operation and maintenance of the facility; (d) provide manpower complement to operationalize all idle and unutilized equipment and procure additional units of baling machines; (e) provide an entrance/exit gate and perimeter fence for the dump site in order to control the entry of unauthorized garbage trucks or individuals; (f) require Barangay Poblacion to relocate the existing MRF to another location which is not prone to flooding; (g)  install control measures in regulating wastes collection at source and from barangays in order  to reduce the volume of wastes at a manageable level; (h) install control mechanisms from transport to the final disposal  to determine the correctness of wastes disposal; (i) submit the ten-year solid waste management plan; and (j) strictly monitor the places where indiscriminate dumping of wastes are rampant and install appropriate controls accordingly.

18.8   We recommended that the MDRRMO and the LDRRM Council take the necessary action for the proper and timely recommendation to the SB on the declaration of a state of calamity if warranted by the situation to immediately respond to disaster victims in order to save lives and property. In case there is a declaration of a state of calamity by the SB, the corresponding lifting under appropriate circumstances thereafter, shall also be issued.

19.10    We recommended that the MSWDO conduct  actual validation of the families and individuals in the affected barangays to come up with the victims’ profiles containing updated and realistic data to become the basis in next year’s planning activities to ensure adequate  and appropriate procurement of  relief goods on a timely basis and thus, properly address the needs of disaster victims.

20.5    We recommended that the concerned offices namely the MSWDO and the MDRRMO immediately inform the Municipal Treasurer or his authorized representative on the existence of donations in-kind to properly acknowledge the receipt thereof. Likewise, proper reporting, accounting, and recording of accountability for donated goods should be made by the respective offices.

21.4    We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustment in the books to reclassify the Extrication Rescue Kit to Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment.
22.5    We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to request the Official receipt and the Report on the Receipt and Utilization of Cash Donations from the Donee-LGUs.

23.9     We recommended that the (a) BUB Focal Person of the respective programs of the LGU (i) strictly monitor the  implementation of targeted projects and take note of any gap for future inputs; (ii) determine reasons for  the non-receipt of the funds from the NGAs concerned in order to determine the next steps to be taken by  the LGU in case the project is  no longer viable; (b) MSWDO prepare the profiling of intended beneficiaries before the project proposal is finalized/submitted for approval of the LCE; and ; (c) Human Resources Management Officer to  augment  personnel in the MSWDO in order to update the databank of residents/ intended beneficiaries per category or classification as to economic condition for easy identification of program beneficiaries.

24.4     We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer continue to be compliant with GSIS regulations on deduction of premium contributions and remittance thereof within the prescribed period.
25.4     We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer continue to be compliant with the provisions of BIR regulations on the remittance of taxes within the prescribed period.

We recommended that  the  Management,  particularly  the  Inventory
Committee reconcile the results  of  the  count  with  the  property  and
accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI  and  RPCPPE
to the Audit Team.

       c. strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids  and

       d.  comply  strictly  with  the  requirements  of  R.A.  No.   9184

We have also audited the payments made  in  CY  2016  for  two  (2)
Infrastructure Projects of the municipality and observed that there  were
deficiencies in the documents attached to the advance  payment,  progress
billings and release of retention money.


Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 provides that claims against government
funds shall be supported with complete documentation.


Consistent thereto, Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160 provides that
it is the duty of the Municipal Accountant to:


“(5) Review supporting documents before preparation of vouchers  to
determine completeness of requirements.”


Items 9.1 and 9.1.1 of COA Circular No.  2012-001  dated  June  14,
2012  provide  the  basic  and  additional  requirements  common  to  all
infrastructure transactions while Items 9.1.1.1 and  9.1.1.3  to  9.1.1.5
thereof provide  the  additional  documentary  requirements  for  advance
payment, progress payment, final payment and release of retention money.

A.  Payments  for  the  Construction/Improvement  of  Riprap   in   Brgy.
  Concepcion Norte in the amount of P9,898,028.01

     1. DV No. 100-1604-363
         Check No. 321555 dated April 11, 2016 P1,484,704.20
         Particulars: payment of 15% mobilization

       a.    The surety bond submitted by the contractor  is  deficient  by
         P494,901.40. Examination  of  the  Surety  Bond  attached  to  the
         disbursement voucher revealed that only P989,802.80 is covered  by
         the surety bond, contrary to Item 9.1.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-
         001 dated June 14, 2012 and Item 4.2 of Annex E of the Handbook on
         Philippine Government Procurement, Sixth Edition,  2012  embodying
         the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, which provide that:


              “The advance payment shall be made only upon  the  submission
            to and acceptance by the procuring  entity  of  an  irrevocable
            standby letter of credit of equivalent value from a  commercial
            bank, a bank guarantee or a surety bond callable  upon  demand,
            issued by a surety or insurance company duly  licensed  by  the
            Insurance Commission and confirmed by  the  procuring  entity.”
            Emphasis supplied


              Moreover, the  concerned  officers  failed  to  examine  the
         content of the surety bond specifically the conditions  stipulated
         that it will guarantee the repair of any  latent  defects  in  the
         completed project within 1 year from its final acceptance.


              The irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value
         from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee or a surety bond callable
         upon demand required under Item 4.2 of Annex E of the Revised  IRR
         is to serve as security for the 15% advance  payment  and  not  to
         guarantee repair of any defects in the completed project within  1
         year from its final acceptance which will be covered by a warranty
         security.

         Check No. 322395 dated November 23, 2016 P320,672.22

         P342,050.37


       a. The date when the Contractor’s Affidavit on payment  of  laborers
         and materials was executed was not indicated.
       b. No attached Clearance from  the  Provincial  Treasurer  that  the
         corresponding sand and gravel fees have been paid.


  Management’s Comments


       The Management in their letter  dated  May  8,  2017  attached  the
  letter request dated October 4, 2016 of the contractor. They  also  added
  that clearance from Provincial Treasurer for the corresponding  sand  and
  gravel was not required since it was automatically deducted in their last
  payment. Since it was not yet remitted to the  Provincial  Treasurer  the
  contractor  could  not  yet  secure  a  clearance  from  the   Provincial
  Treasurer. They further averred  that  there  is  no  test  required  for
  riprap.


  Auditor’s Rejoinder


       Perusal of the letter dated  October  4,  2016  of  the  contractor
  revealed that it pertains to the request for the release of final payment
  and not for the fourth partial payment which is noted to  be  lacking  as
  discussed above.


       During the exit conference, the Audit Team  also  advised  them  to
  require the contractor to pay instead of deducting the corresponding fees
  in the last payment so that it could be remitted early and the  clearance
  could be properly secured from the  Provincial  Treasurer.  On  the  test
  analysis, the Audit Team informed them that it will be  referred  to  the
  COA TAS.


B. Payments for the project Design and Build for Expansion of Sta.  Maria
  Level III Water System with a total contract amount of P13,426,000.00


       On October 28, 2015, the municipality represented by the  Municipal
  Mayor  entered  into  a  Contract  Agreement  with  South  Builders   and
  Construction Supplies represented by Mr. Alfredo M. Lopez for the  Design
  and Build for Expansion of Sta. Maria Level III Water System with a total
  contract amount of P13,426,000.00.  Deficiencies noted in the  review  of
  the said contract were communicated to the management by the  Audit  Team
  thru issuance of Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2015-016 dated November
  16, 2015 and were incorporated in the CY 2015 Annual Audit Report of  the
  municipality.


        In CY 2016, payments of progress billings and release of retention
  money were made by the municipality.  Verification of the  payments  made
  revealed various deficiencies, contrary to the provision of Section 4 (6)
  of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated  June
  14,  2012, to wit:


     1. DV No. 100-1603-200
       Check No. 321406 dated March 1, 2016 P4,591,953.26
       Particulars:  first  partial   payment   with   gross   amount   of
       P7,535,939.47


  a. There was no result of test analysis attached.
  b. The date when the Contractor’s affidavit on payment  of  laborers  and
     materials was executed was not indicated.
  c. The pictures attached were not properly labeled as to  items  of  work
     and dates when these were taken.


     2. DV No. 100-1604-385
       Check No. 321571 dated April 18, 2016 P1,938,860.92
       Particulars:  second  partial  payment   with   gross   amount   of
       P2,462,046.81


       a. No result of test analysis attached.
       b. No pictures, before, during and after construction of  items  of
          work were attached.
       c.  Photocopy  of  vouchers  of  all  previous  payments  were  not
          attached.
       d. The date when the Contractor’s affidavit on payment of  laborers
          and materials was executed was not indicated.
       e. A copy of  a  letter-request  from  the  representative  of  the
          contractor for time extension dated June 6, 2016  was  furnished
          to the Audit Team. There was no information written on the  face
          of the letter as to when this  was  received  by  the  concerned
          office thus, the Audit Team could not determine  if  the  agency
          was given ample time to investigate and decide whether to  grant
          the request.


                Additionally, it was stated in the  letter  that  the  pipe
          laying activities were stopped by a landowner  and  since  there
          was no assurance that a right of way will be donated,  the  pipe
          laying was rerouted with the approval of  the  Municipal  Mayor.
          However, copy  of  the  document  showing  that  said  rerouting
          activity was duly approved was not furnished to the Audit Team.

     3. DV No. 100-1610-1104
       Check No. 322271 dated October 13, 2016 P179,360.82
       Particulars:  third  and  final  payment  with  gross   amount   of
       P3,428,013.72


  a.  No letter from the contractor for the request of  final  payment  was
     attached.
  b. No result of test analysis attached.
  c. Photocopy of vouchers of all previous payments were not attached.
  d. No As-Built plans attached.
  e. No clearance from the Provincial Treasurer that the corresponding sand

  f. As discussed in Observation No. 2 (e), the  contractor  requested  for

     request was granted but based on the other  documents  attached,  time
     extension of 60 days or until August 24, 2016 was granted.


                 Relative to that, another  letter  was  submitted  by  the

          her and the LGU allowing the contractor to cross  her  property.
          However, the letter was dated August 25, 2016 or one  day  after

                   Granting  of  time  extension  despite  failure  of   the
          contractor to provide a notice relative to the extension of time
          prior to the expiration of the  contract  time  is  contrary  to
          Annex E, Item 10.1 of the 2016 Revised  Implementing  Rules  and

  g. No attached copy of turn-over of  documents/transfer  of  project  and
     facilities such as motor vehicle,  laptops,  or  other  equipment  and
     furniture included in the contract to Municipality of Santa Maria,  if
     any.

     4. DV No. 100-1610-1105
       Check No. 322272 dated October 13, 2016 P1,342,600.00
       Particulars: Release of Retention Money


             Release of retention money was not supported with  a  request
       from the contractor for the substitution  of  the  retention  money
       with a surety bond. Moreover,  the  concerned  officers  failed  to
       examine the content of the surety bond submitted by the  contractor
       specifically the conditions stipulated that it will  guarantee  the
       repair of any latent defects in the completed project within 1 year
       from its final  acceptance.  Said  condition  is  contrary  to  the
       provision of Section 6.2 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of R.A.  No.
       9184 that surety bond to be posted in favor of the government shall

Direct the Accountant to make the necessary correcting/adjusting entries in the books to record the procurement of supplies and materials to its appropriate inventory accounts. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to stamp “PAID” the Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting documents to avoid the risk of double payments and to comply with COA Circular No. 92-389 to safeguard government resources.
